findings.title: Optional chain on .state contradicts the declared type
findings.detail: BotDetail.state is typed as required (state: BotState, no ?) in trading.ts line 93. The fix adds ?.state? at all 9 call sites, which the TypeScript checker accepts silently — optional chaining a non-optional member is legal but structurally a no-op from the compiler perspective. The mismatch means one of two things is true: (a) the API can actually return a BotDetail without state and the type is wrong, or (b) the optional chain is defensive noise added to suppress a runtime crash whose real cause was something else. In case (a) the type needs to be updated to state?: BotState so the compiler enforces nullability downstream and callers cannot silently drop required fallbacks. In case (b) the root cause (why .state was ever undefined at runtime) is undiagnosed and masked. Neither path was resolved — the type and the chain are now inconsistent with each other.
findings.recommendation: Determine whether the API can return {config, state: undefined}. If yes, update BotDetail.state to state?: BotState so the type is accurate and the compiler enforces the guards. If no, remove the ?.state? chains and investigate the actual crash trigger (likely a race where detail is non-null but arrived mid-update, e.g. from a hot-reload or stale closure).

findings.title: fetchBotDetail casts raw axios response to BotDetail with no runtime validation
findings.detail: Line 26 returns res.data cast from any to BotDetail with no Zod, io-ts, or manual shape guard. If the backend returns a shape inconsistent with BotDetail (missing state key, wrong field types), the type system gives no warning and the component receives a structurally incorrect object. This is a plausible origin of the crash that F32 addressed — a runtime shape mismatch the type layer cannot detect.
findings.recommendation: Add a lightweight runtime shape check in fetchBotDetail, or at minimum an assertion that res.data.state is present before returning. Low priority unless the F32 crash recurs.

residual_risks: If BotDetail.state is genuinely nullable at runtime (e.g. a timing window between bot creation and first state flush), the fix is correct behavior but the type is a lie. Future code that accesses detail.state without an optional chain will silently crash in that window because the type gives false safety. This risk is live until the type is corrected or the window is closed.
testing_gaps: No test covers the render path where detail is non-null but detail.state is missing/undefined — the exact scenario F32 was meant to guard against. BotCard.test.tsx should include a case where fetchBotDetail resolves to {config: ..., state: undefined} to confirm the optional chains produce the expected fallback values rather than a blank render.
